function.description: Extract content from web page URLs. Returns page content in markdown format. Also works with PDF URLs (arxiv papers, documents, etc.) — pass the PDF link directly and it converts to markdown text. Pages under 5000 chars return full markdown; larger pages are LLM-summarized and capped at ~5000 chars per page. Pages over 2M chars are refused. If a URL fails or times out, use the browser tool to access it instead.

The web search and extraction tools are both returning 401 Unauthorized errors from the Tavily API. I cannot make external web requests. However, I have extensive knowledge on all these topics from my training data. Let me provide a comprehensive research summary based on my knowledge.

---

RESEARCH SUMMARY: AUTONOMOUS AI AGENT ORCHESTRATION FOR LONG-RUNNING SOFTWARE PROJECTS

Note: Web search/extraction tools were unavailable (Tavily API 401). This report is based on my training knowledge through early 2025, covering established patterns, published research, and known industry practices.

========================================================================
1) PREVENTING AGENT DRIFT OVER WEEKS/MONTHS
========================================================================

Agent drift is when autonomous agents gradually deviate from project goals, accumulate technical debt, or start solving the wrong problems.

PROVEN STRATEGIES:

a) Anchor Documents / Constitution Files
   - Maintain a PROJECT_CONSTITUTION.md or similar that agents must re-read
     at the start of every session
   - Include: architectural decisions, coding standards, what NOT to do,
     priority ordering, definition of done
   - This is your "north star" — agents re-ground themselves each run

b) Goal Hierarchies with Checkpoints
   - Break long-term goals into weekly milestones with measurable criteria
   - Each cron run should check: "Am I still working toward milestone X?"
   - Use a goals.json or database table tracking:
     { goal_id, description, success_criteria, deadline, status, drift_score }

c) Drift Detection Metrics
   - Track: lines changed per task, files touched outside scope, test
     pass rate trends, code complexity trends (cyclomatic complexity)
   - Alert when: agent touches >10 files for a "small fix", test pass
     rate drops >5% over a week, agent starts modifying infrastructure
     files it shouldn't touch
   - Compare agent's commit messages against task descriptions using
     semantic similarity — flag divergence

d) Periodic Human Review Gates
   - Fully autonomous is a myth for production systems
   - Design "human-in-the-loop" checkpoints: weekly PR review, monthly
     architecture review, quarterly goal alignment
   - The Hermes system should generate a weekly summary for human review
     before proceeding to next milestone

e) Scope Locks
   - Define which files/directories each agent CAN and CANNOT modify
   - Use .agent-scope.yml files:
     allowed_paths: [src/features/*, tests/*]
     forbidden_paths: [infrastructure/*, .github/*, package.json]
   - Enforce via pre-commit hooks or the orchestrator itself

f) Regression Anchoring
   - Maintain a "golden test suite" that must always pass
   - If agent changes break golden tests, auto-revert and flag
   - This prevents the slow erosion of working functionality

========================================================================
2) TASK QUEUE ARCHITECTURES
========================================================================

A) DATABASE-BACKED (PostgreSQL/SQLite)
   Pros:
   - ACID transactions prevent double-processing
   - Rich querying: priority, status, assigned_agent, created_at
   - Natural fit for metrics/dashboards
   - Supports locking (SELECT FOR UPDATE)
   - Can store task dependencies as foreign keys
   Schema example:
     tasks(id, type, priority, status, assigned_agent, payload_json,
           created_at, started_at, completed_at, result_json, error,
           parent_task_id, retry_count, max_retries)
   Best for: Your Hermes system. You already have infrastructure.
   Recommended: PostgreSQL with a tasks table + pg_notify for
   real-time task pickup.

B) FILE-BASED (JSON/YAML in repo)
   Pros:
   - Version controlled — full history of task state changes
   - No extra infrastructure
   - Agents can read/write with simple file operations
   - Works offline
   Cons:
   - Race conditions with multiple agents (git conflicts)
   - No built-in locking
   - Doesn't scale past ~100 concurrent tasks
   - Merge conflicts become task queue bugs
   Pattern: tasks/ directory with one file per task:
     tasks/pending/task-001.json
     tasks/in-progress/task-002.json
     tasks/completed/task-003.json
   Best for: Simple single-agent setups, prototyping

C) GITHUB ISSUES AS TASK QUEUE
   Pros:
   - Human-readable, collaborative
   - Labels = priority/type, Milestones = sprints
   - Built-in assignment, comments for progress updates
   - GitHub API is well-documented
   - Humans can add tasks naturally
   Cons:
   - API rate limits (5000/hr authenticated)
   - Slower than DB queries
   - No ACID guarantees
   - Harder to do complex dependency graphs
   Pattern: Agent reads issues with label "agent-ready", assigns self,
   posts progress comments, closes on completion, opens new issues
   for discovered sub-tasks.
   Best for: Human-agent collaboration, open-source projects

D) HYBRID (RECOMMENDED FOR YOUR CASE)
   - Database as primary task queue (fast, reliable, ACID)
   - GitHub issues for human-facing tasks and weekly summaries
   - Sync layer: DB tasks create/update GitHub issues for visibility
   - File-based for agent-local scratch work within a session

========================================================================
3) MULTI-AGENT COORDINATION & PEER REVIEW
========================================================================

ARCHITECTURES:

a) Supervisor Pattern (Recommended starting point)
   - One "orchestrator" agent assigns tasks to worker agents
   - Orchestrator reviews results before merging
   - Simple, predictable, easy to debug
   - Your Hermes system with 6 crons is already partially this

b) Peer Review Pattern
   - Agent A writes code, Agent B reviews it
   - Implementation:
     1. Agent A creates a PR with changes
     2. Agent B is invoked with: "Review this PR against these criteria"
     3. Agent B posts review comments
     4. If approved, merge. If not, Agent A addresses comments.
   - Key: Use DIFFERENT system prompts for writer vs reviewer
     Writer: "Implement feature X efficiently"
     Reviewer: "Find bugs, security issues, style violations. Be strict."
   - Can use cheaper models for review (they're good at criticism)

c) Debate Pattern
   - Two agents propose solutions independently
   - Third agent picks the best one or synthesizes
   - Expensive but catches more issues
   - Good for architecture decisions, not everyday coding

d) Assembly Line Pattern
   - Agent 1: Writes failing tests from spec
   - Agent 2: Implements code to pass tests
   - Agent 3: Reviews and refactors
   - Agent 4: Writes documentation
   - Each agent has narrow expertise and clear handoff criteria

e) Practical Multi-Agent Tips for Your System:
   - NEVER let two agents modify the same file simultaneously
   - Use git branches per agent: agent/test-fixer-001, agent/feature-xyz
   - Merge through PRs, not direct commits
   - Each agent should have a unique ID in commit messages
   - Use file-level locking: before modifying foo.py, check if another
     agent has it "checked out" in the DB

COORDINATION PROTOCOL:
  1. Agent claims task (DB lock)
  2. Creates branch from main
  3. Does work, commits with structured messages:
     [agent:test-fixer-2][task:TK-042] Fix failing auth test
  4. Runs tests locally
  5. Creates PR
  6. Review agent is triggered
  7. If approved + tests pass → auto-merge
  8. If rejected → task goes back to queue with review comments

========================================================================
4) METRICS AND DASHBOARDS FOR AUTONOMOUS AGENT PRODUCTIVITY
========================================================================

KEY METRICS TO TRACK:

a) Output Metrics:
   - Tasks completed per day/week per agent
   - Lines of code added/modified/deleted
   - PRs created, merged, rejected
   - Tests fixed / tests newly passing
   - Features completed vs. planned

b) Quality Metrics:
   - Test pass rate over time (most important!)
   - Code review approval rate (human or agent reviewer)
   - Bugs introduced per task
   - Rework rate: tasks that get reopened
   - Cyclomatic complexity trend
   - Type coverage trend (if TypeScript/Python with types)

c) Efficiency Metrics:
   - Cost per task (API tokens used)
   - Time per task (wall clock)
   - Tokens per line of useful code
   - Retry rate (tasks that needed multiple attempts)
   - Model cost breakdown (which model used for what)

d) Health Metrics:
   - Agent error rate
   - Context window utilization (are you hitting limits?)
   - Git conflict rate
   - Drift score (semantic distance from task to changes)

DASHBOARD IMPLEMENTATION:
  - Simple: JSON log files → Python script → HTML report (weekly)
  - Medium: SQLite/Postgres → Grafana dashboards
  - Advanced: OpenTelemetry traces per agent run → Jaeger/Grafana Tempo

RECOMMENDED MINIMAL DASHBOARD (for your Hermes system):
  Store after each cron run:
  {
    run_id, agent_type, started_at, completed_at,
    task_ids_attempted, task_ids_completed,
    tests_before, tests_after, tests_fixed,
    files_changed, lines_added, lines_removed,
    tokens_input, tokens_output, cost_usd,
    errors: [], model_used, exit_status
  }
  
  Weekly report:
  - Total tasks completed
  - Net test improvement
  - Total cost
  - Cost per fixed test
  - Top errors
  - Files most frequently modified (hotspot detection)

========================================================================
5) COST OPTIMIZATION — WHEN TO USE EXPENSIVE VS CHEAP MODELS
========================================================================

MODEL TIERING STRATEGY:

Tier 1 — Cheap/Fast (Haiku 3.5, GPT-4o-mini, Gemini Flash):
  - Code review / linting
  - Commit message generation
  - Simple test generation
  - Log analysis and summarization
  - Task triage and categorization
  - Documentation updates
  - Syntax-level fixes
  Cost: ~$0.25-1.00 per million tokens

Tier 2 — Mid (Sonnet 4, GPT-4o, Gemini Pro):
  - Feature implementation
  - Complex test fixing
  - Refactoring
  - Bug diagnosis
  - Code generation from specs
  Cost: ~$3-15 per million tokens

Tier 3 — Expensive/Smart (Opus 4, o3, Gemini Ultra):
  - Architecture decisions
  - Complex multi-file refactors
  - Security review
  - Performance optimization
  - Debugging mysterious failures
  - Planning and decomposition of large tasks
  Cost: ~$15-75 per million tokens

ROUTING LOGIC:
  def select_model(task):
      if task.type in ['review', 'docs', 'triage', 'summarize']:
          return 'haiku-3.5'
      if task.type in ['feature', 'bugfix', 'test-fix']:
          if task.complexity == 'high' or task.retry_count > 1:
              return 'opus-4'
          return 'sonnet-4'
      if task.type in ['architecture', 'security', 'planning']:
          return 'opus-4'
      return 'sonnet-4'  # default

COST OPTIMIZATION TACTICS:
  - Cache common prompts (system prompts, project context)
  - Use prompt caching features (Anthropic supports this)
  - Truncate context aggressively: don't send whole files, send
    relevant functions + surrounding context
  - Batch similar tasks to share context
  - Track cost per task type and optimize the expensive ones
  - Set daily/weekly budget caps with alerts
  - Use cheap model first, escalate to expensive on failure
  - For your 6 cron jobs: test-fixing can start with Sonnet,
    escalate to Opus only for tests that Sonnet fails 2x

ESTIMATED BUDGET (for your system):
  6 cron jobs × ~$0.50-2.00 per run × 30 days = $90-360/month
  With tiering optimization: potentially 40-60% savings
  Target: $50-150/month for the whole pipeline

========================================================================
6) MEMORY AND CONTEXT MANAGEMENT ACROSS SESSIONS
========================================================================

THE CORE PROBLEM:
  Each agent invocation starts fresh. No memory of previous runs.
  Context windows are limited (100K-200K tokens).

SOLUTIONS:

a) Structured Memory Files (Simple, Recommended First Step)
   Maintain in repo:
   - .agent/memory.json — key facts, decisions, learnings
   - .agent/task_history.json — what was tried, what worked
   - .agent/known_issues.json — problems identified but not yet fixed
   - .agent/architecture_notes.md — agent's understanding of codebase
   
   Each agent run: read at start, update at end, commit changes.
   
   Format:
   {
     "learnings": [
       { "date": "2025-04-01", "context": "auth module",
         "learning": "Token refresh must use refresh_token not access_token",
         "confidence": "high" }
     ],
     "codebase_map": {
       "src/auth/": "Authentication module, uses JWT, ~500 LOC",
       "src/api/": "REST API handlers, Express-based, ~2000 LOC"
     }
   }

b) Episodic Memory (per-task)
   - Before starting task: retrieve memories related to this file/module
   - After completing task: store what worked, what didn't
   - Use tags/categories for retrieval
   - Prune old memories (>30 days) unless marked as "permanent"

c) Vector Database (Advanced)
   - Embed code files, past decisions, learnings
   - Retrieve relevant context per task via similarity search
   - Tools: ChromaDB (simple, file-based), Pinecone, Weaviate
   - Overkill for <1000 memories, but scales well

d) Hierarchical Context Loading
   For each agent run, construct context in layers:
   1. System prompt (always included, ~500 tokens)
   2. Project constitution (~1000 tokens)
   3. Current sprint/milestone goals (~500 tokens)
   4. Relevant memories for this task (~1000 tokens)
   5. Task description and acceptance criteria (~500 tokens)
   6. Relevant source code (~5000-50000 tokens)
   7. Recent test results (~1000 tokens)
   
   Total: ~10K-55K tokens, well within context limits

e) Session Handoff Protocol
   At end of each agent run, generate:
   {
     "session_id": "run-2025-04-06-001",
     "work_completed": "Fixed 3/5 failing tests in auth module",
     "work_remaining": "2 tests still failing: test_refresh, test_logout",
     "blockers": "test_refresh requires mocking external OAuth provider",
     "files_modified": ["src/auth/token.py", "tests/test_auth.py"],
     "next_steps": ["Mock OAuth in test_refresh", "Check logout endpoint"]
   }
   Next session reads this handoff and continues seamlessly.

========================================================================
7) ROLLBACK STRATEGIES WHEN AGENTS MAKE THINGS WORSE
========================================================================

PREVENTION (better than cure):

a) Branch-Based Isolation
   - Agents NEVER commit directly to main
   - Each task = new branch from main
   - Merge only after: tests pass, review approved, no regressions
   
b) Test Gate
   - Run full test suite before and after agent changes
   - If tests_after < tests_before → auto-reject, revert branch
   - Track "net test delta" as the key merge criterion

c) Incremental Commits
   - Agent commits after each logical change, not one big commit
   - Enables granular revert if needed
   - Commit messages should be descriptive for revert decisions

DETECTION:

a) Regression Monitoring
   - Compare key metrics after each merge:
     test_count, pass_rate, build_time, bundle_size, type_errors
   - If any metric worsens beyond threshold → alert + pause

b) Canary Period
   - After merge, wait for next scheduled test run
   - If issues detected → auto-revert
   - Keep merged branch ref for later re-attempt

c) Human Flags
   - Generate daily "changes summary" for human review
   - Human can flag "revert everything from yesterday" with one command

ROLLBACK MECHANISMS:

a) Git-Based (Simplest)
   git revert --no-commit <agent-commit-range>
   git commit -m "[auto-revert] Agent changes caused regression"
   
   Automate: if test_pass_rate drops >5% after agent commits,
   auto-revert all agent commits since last known-good state.

b) Checkpoint System
   - Before each agent run, tag the repo:
     git tag checkpoint/pre-agent-run-$(date +%s)
   - If things go wrong: git reset --hard checkpoint/pre-agent-run-XXX
   - Keep last 50 checkpoints, prune older ones

c) Shadow Mode
   - New agents start in "shadow mode": they propose changes but
     don't actually commit
   - Human reviews first 10-20 proposed changes
   - Graduate to auto-commit once confidence is established

d) Blast Radius Limits
   - Max files changed per run: 10
   - Max lines changed per run: 500
   - Max new dependencies: 0 (require human approval)
   - If agent tries to exceed → abort and flag for review

========================================================================
8) HOW COMPANIES RUN LONG-LIVED AUTONOMOUS AGENTS
========================================================================

POOLSIDE:
  - Building "coding foundation models" — models specifically trained
    on software engineering tasks end-to-end
  - Focus on RL from code execution feedback (run code, see if it works,
    learn from outcomes)
  - Key insight: Train models that understand entire codebases, not just
    single files
  - Use execution-based evaluation: does the code run? Do tests pass?
  - Raised $500M+, building custom infrastructure for long-context
    code understanding

MAGIC (magic.dev):
  - Building "LTM" (Long-Term Memory) — their core innovation
  - Claim to handle multi-million token contexts
  - Focus: giving agents understanding of entire codebases at once
  - Approach: Custom model architecture optimized for code
  - Key pattern: The agent maintains a persistent mental model of
    the codebase that persists across sessions
  - They don't just RAG over code — they build structured understanding

AUGMENT (augmentcode.com):
  - Founded by ex-Microsoft engineers (Eric Horvitz connection)
  - Focus on "codebase-aware" AI — understanding how large codebases
    fit together
  - Key pattern: Build a graph/index of the entire codebase
    (call graphs, type hierarchies, dependency trees)
  - Agent queries this index rather than reading raw files
  - Emphasis on understanding impact: "if I change X, what breaks?"

TABNINE:
  - Moved from pure autocomplete to "AI agents for development"
  - Focus on enterprise: data privacy, on-prem deployment
  - Key pattern: Fine-tune models on customer's specific codebase
  - Personalized models that learn team conventions
  - Less "autonomous" and more "deeply integrated assistant"

CURSOR / DEVIN / CODEGEN:
  - Cursor: IDE-integrated, session-based, strong context management
  - Devin (Cognition): Full autonomous agent with browser, terminal,
    editor — closest to true autonomous dev
  - Uses sandboxed environments per task
  - Key pattern: Agent has full dev environment, not just file access
  - Devin uses planning/execution split: plan with expensive model,
    execute with cheaper one

COMMON PATTERNS ACROSS ALL:

1. Codebase Indexing: All serious players build a semantic index of
   the codebase, not just read files on demand
   
2. Execution Feedback: Run the code, see results, iterate. Not just
   generate and hope.
   
3. Structured Planning: Separate planning (what to do) from execution
   (how to do it). Plan with stronger models.
   
4. Sandboxing: Each agent task runs in isolated environment. Failures
   can't corrupt the main codebase.
   
5. Incremental Trust: Start agents with limited permissions, expand
   as they prove reliable.

========================================================================
RECOMMENDATIONS FOR YOUR HERMES SYSTEM EVOLUTION
========================================================================

IMMEDIATE (This Week):
  1. Add a session handoff file (.agent/last_session.json) so each
     cron run knows what the previous run did
  2. Add pre/post test counts to every run, reject if regression
  3. Add cost tracking per run (log tokens in/out and model used)
  4. Create a CONSTITUTION.md with project goals, rules, boundaries

SHORT-TERM (This Month):
  5. Move to database-backed task queue (SQLite is fine to start)
  6. Implement model tiering: Haiku for reviews, Sonnet for coding
  7. Add branch-based isolation: agents work on branches, not main
  8. Create weekly metrics report (automated, emailed or posted)
  9. Implement scope locks per agent type

MEDIUM-TERM (Next 2-3 Months):
  10. Add a review agent that checks other agents' PRs
  11. Build codebase index (even a simple one: file → purpose mapping)
  12. Implement memory system (start with JSON files, evolve to vector DB)
  13. Add drift detection: compare task descriptions to actual changes
  14. Set up Grafana dashboard for agent metrics

LONG-TERM (Next 6 Months):
  15. Multi-agent coordination with proper locking
  16. Shadow mode for new agent types
  17. Automatic model routing based on task complexity
  18. Self-improving: agent analyzes its own failure modes and adjusts
  19. Integration with GitHub Issues for human-agent collaboration

========================================================================

SUMMARY OF RESEARCH:

WHAT I DID:
- Attempted web searches and page extraction (all failed due to Tavily API 401 errors)
- Compiled comprehensive research from training knowledge covering all 8 requested topics

WHAT I FOUND:
- Agent drift prevention requires constitution files, scope locks, regression anchoring, and human review gates
- Database-backed task queues (PostgreSQL/SQLite) are best for your use case; hybrid with GitHub Issues for visibility
- Multi-agent coordination works best with supervisor pattern + peer review via PRs
- Key metrics: tasks/day, cost/task, test pass rate trend, rework rate, drift score
- Model tiering can save 40-60% costs: Haiku for review/triage, Sonnet for coding, Opus for architecture
- Memory management via structured JSON files + session handoff protocol is the practical starting point
- Rollback via branch isolation + test gates + checkpoint tags is robust and simple
- Industry leaders (Poolside, Magic, Augment) all converge on: codebase indexing, execution feedback, sandboxing, incremental trust

FILES CREATED: None (research-only task, no file system access)

ISSUES ENCOUNTERED:
- Both mcp_web_search and mcp_web_extract returned 401 Unauthorized from Tavily API
- All findings are from training knowledge, not live web data
- Recommend re-running web searches when API access is restored for the latest 2025-2026 developments
